petitioner_background: The petitioner is a woman variously identified as a journalist and 'patron of the arts' who sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under INA § 203(b)(1)(A) (EB-1A). She worked primarily as a journalist from 1987 to 2013, reportedly for an Italian public broadcasting company based in Italy covering stories in the United States, and produced two shows on Broadway in 2012 and 2014. She co-founded a foundation/award to honor distinguished writers, poets and artists, and also holds a doctorate in Architecture from an Italian university. She stated she was unable to sustain her work after a serious accident in 2015.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted press passes establishing her employment as a journalist with an Italian public broadcasting company, based in Italy, with former colleagues attesting that she covered a range of stories and events in the United States. One colleague claimed she 'organize[d] ... the correspondent's office,' though this individual was a newspaper reporter without direct knowledge. She also submitted letters from colleagues indicating she served as director of a School of Journalism in Italy, with one colleague saying she became director after serving on juries to select students, and another asserting she actually founded the school. Additional letters came from writers at a School of Journalism at a university, and from a manager of merchandising and a faculty restaurant who stated he worked at the school from 1992 to 1996.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO disagreed with the Director's finding. It noted that the role of a network journalist is not automatically leading or critical, particularly given the petitioner's own assertion that '1600 journalist[s] work at [the network] full time.' The colleague who described her role as organizing the correspondent's office was a newspaper reporter without direct knowledge. The evidence did not establish she was an anchor rather than a correspondent. Regarding the School of Journalism, the AAO found apparent inconsistencies in the letters (one said she became director after serving on juries, another said she founded the school), noted the petitioner did not mention the school at all in her initial submission, and found no documentary evidence about the school or her role there. The letters also created confusion between two similarly-named schools. Given the vague and inconsistent claims and lack of verifiable documentary evidence, the AAO concluded she did not meet her burden.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner initially cited her co-creation of a foundation/award under this criterion. In a supplemental submission, she indicated she participated as a judge in the field of journalism, submitting three letters stating she served on a jury that selected students to attend the School of Journalism. She also submitted a translated copy of an article from an unidentified publication indicating she sat on a jury to decide the winner of an award for 'articles, interventions or essays on the topic Constitutional reforms: rules to be retained.' The article stated the award ceremony was held on 3/24/97 but bore a dateline of May 1996.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO disagreed with the Director. Regarding co-creation of the award, the petitioner did not explain how this showed her participation as a judge, and the record was ambiguous as to whether she participated as one of the judges who chose recipients. The awards went to 'writers, poets and artists,' fields she did not claim to work in, and she did not explain how they were allied to her own fields. Regarding the jury service selecting students, the letters were vague and inconsistent, and there was no documentary evidence from the School of Journalism, an omission of particular concern because the petitioner initially did not mention the school at all. Regarding the constitutional reforms article, the mismatched dates (3/24/97 ceremony vs. May 1996 dateline) appeared in both the translation and the Italian original, which came from no identified source other than an email from the petitioner. Citing Matter of Ho, the AAO found these inconsistencies and deficiencies diminished evidentiary weight, and held the evidence was insufficient.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner cited the following as qualifying memberships: a doctorate in Architecture from an Italian university; an assistant professorship in the department of Architecture at a university; listings in the Italian Register of Professional Architects and the Italian Register of Professional Journalists. She submitted an unsigned letter attributed to a former dean of the university listing her achievements and expressing the opinion that she qualified as extraordinary ability. She asserted her former employer (the Italian broadcasting network) has a distinguished reputation and that one of its offices 'is the most prestigious.' On appeal, she contended her involvement with the Foundation she co-founded was itself a qualifying membership, and asserted that 'there are professional associations with different levels of membership and higher levels of membership may satisfy this evidentiary criterion.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet the plain language of this criterion. Most of the cited information related to architecture, but the record did not indicate she sought classification in that field or intended to work as an architect. She did not explain how a university degree and faculty position constitute membership in associations, or establish the role recognized experts play in degree-granting and hiring processes. She did not establish the requirements for listing in the two Italian Registers or show that listed individuals collectively comprise an association. The unsigned letter from the former dean did not mention membership in any association. She submitted no evidence that employment by the broadcasting network amounted to association membership contingent on outstanding achievements judged by recognized experts. As to the Foundation she co-founded, she did not show her ability to co-found it required nationally or internationally recognized experts to judge her achievements as outstanding, nor did she identify those achievements or experts. Her general statement about tiered memberships was not tied to any specific claimed membership.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner contended her role in co-creating the award/prize was an original artistic contribution of major significance and that the award was 'the Italian equivalent of the Nobel Prize in Literature.' She submitted letters from and background information about several award recipients, including a former U.S. Poet Laureate who stated the award was 'one of the two or three most significant forms of recognition I have received,' citing 'the international nature of the prize, the elegance of the presentation, the generous reception ... [and] the sculptural work of art that comes with the prize.' Another former Poet Laureate stated it was 'possibly Italy's most prestigious international literary honor.' On appeal, she submitted two letters from individuals involved with the Nobel Prize for Literature: a university professor 'in continuous contact with the jury' who stated the Swedish Academy 'has taken account of the decisions of the Foundation' with 'direct cooperation ... [since] 2014'; and a member of the Academy and Nobel Committee for Literature who stated the prize 'has an important place in contemporary culture' and had been awarded to several later Nobel laureates. She also submitted a letter from an attorney identified as Italy's Honorary Consul stating her 'contribution is a bridge to the Italian and American cultures' and she acted as a liaison with Italian Diplomats — though the attorney later acknowledged representing her in a personal injury case.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. It cited Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund for the principle that 'major significance' has meaning and is not superfluous. The reputation of the award-winning writers was not in dispute, but their acclaim did not establish the significance of the prize itself. The petitioner provided high-quality documentation about the winners but not about the prize or her creation of it. She compared it to the Nobel Prize but did not show comparable prestige or news coverage — the documented coverage consisted primarily of press releases from winners' employers and publishers. The Nobel-related letters showed some experts were familiar with the prize, but familiarity does not equate to major significance; recognition does not necessarily equate to major significance in the field. The petitioner did not describe the nature of the cooperation with the Swedish Academy, submit documentation of any formal agreement, or show how the Academy selected such cooperating organizations. Furthermore, she did not explain how creating the prize was an 'original' contribution or identify any characteristic distinguishing it from other literary prizes. The Honorary Consul's statements about her bridging cultures did not explain the larger significance, and his objectivity was undermined by his prior representation of her. Without specific evidence showing her work was unusually influential or widely applied throughout her field, she did not meet the criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted she met this criterion because she produced two plays in 2012 and 2014. On appeal, her brief discussed display only in the context of the 'comparable evidence' clause at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), asserting for instance that 'scholars and academics display their work' at academic conferences.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the claim abandoned on appeal. The petitioner did not claim to have met any other regulatory criteria through her work as a theatrical producer and did not explain how that work related to her journalism career or her endeavors relating to the Foundation. Her appellate discussion of display was limited to the comparable evidence clause — which she had not previously invoked — and to disputing Kazarian and related case law, without discussing the specifics of her case. The AAO cited Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen. and Hristov v. Roark for the principle that issues not defended on appeal are abandoned.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner stated that the 'average News Anchor Salary in New York' was '116,923 in 2018 dollars,' and that her '[t]otal gross annual pay in 2000 was $146,530.' She contended that '[i]n today's dollars, the value of the contract would be significantly higher.' Supporting documents showed the 90th percentile of New York news anchor salaries was $427,116 in 2018. A translated letter from the Italian network from May 2000 specified her expected total gross annual pay as 146,530,160 Italian lira, with the same document stating that '[b]y converting into foreign currency,' her yearly pay in US dollars would be $59,140.70.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the claim abandoned on appeal, but noted the petitioner did not accurately describe her own compensation. Even taking the $146,530 figure at face value, it was not significantly high compared to others in the field given the very broad salary range with the 90th percentile at $427,116. More significantly, the petitioner misrepresented her compensation: the actual figure was 146,530,160 Italian lira, which the same document converted to $59,140.70 — not $146,530 in US dollars. She did not show this amount was significantly high for news anchors in New York in 2000 and did not submit any other documentary evidence relating to her compensation. She did not revisit the issue on appeal, and the AAO considered the claim abandoned.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal on de novo review, finding the petitioner did not submit the required initial evidence of either a one-time achievement or documents meeting at least three of the ten criteria. The AAO disagreed with the Director's conclusion that she had met the leading/critical role and judging criteria, and agreed with the Director that she had not met the membership and original contributions criteria, while noting the display and high salary claims were abandoned on appeal. Because the petitioner did not meet the initial evidentiary threshold, a full final merits determination under Kazarian was unnecessary, but the AAO nevertheless noted it had reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that she had established the sustained acclaim and recognition required for the classification. The AAO additionally observed that she had not demonstrated all submitted evidence related to a single field of endeavor, having submitted evidence across journalism, stage production, organizing awards ceremonies, and architecture without identifying a unifying field.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple credibility and procedural concerns were noted. (1) The AAO flagged that arguments disputing case law in the petitioner's brief appeared to have been copied without attribution from an AILA amicus curiae brief filed in an unrelated proceeding, with the phrase 'AILA reiterates its concern ...' appearing on page 16 of the petitioner's brief. (2) The petitioner materially misrepresented her compensation, claiming total gross annual pay of '$146,530' when the underlying document from her Italian employer specified 146,530,160 Italian lira, which the same document itself converted to $59,140.70 in US dollars. (3) Multiple letters from colleagues contained apparent inconsistencies (e.g., whether she founded or merely directed the School of Journalism) and vagueness, and the record contained no documentary evidence from the school despite her claims. (4) An article submitted as judging evidence had mismatched dates (May 1996 dateline but a March 24, 1997 ceremony date) and came from no identified source other than an email from the petitioner herself. (5) A letter supporting her was from an attorney who later acknowledged having represented her in a personal injury case, undermining objectivity. (6) A letter from a former dean supporting her membership claim was unsigned. (7) The petitioner omitted any mention of the School of Journalism in her initial submission, only raising it later. The AAO cited Matter of Ho regarding diminished evidentiary weight from inconsistencies.
